trap_traits.name: Trap statement that sounds like the conclusion but has no support
Main conclusion not explicitly stated in the stimulus
Correct answer doesn't match the conclusion word for word
Wrong answer expresses something the author would agree with — but isn't the conclusion
Correct answer vaguely worded, placed next to an attractive wrong answer
Intermediate Conclusion traps and Cause/Effect traps

named_tools.name: FABS

named_tools.name: 2-Part Conclusion Check
named_tools.what: Verification protocol: (1) Sounds like author's opinion? (2) Supported by at least one explicit claim?

named_tools.name: Upside Down Argument
named_tools.what: LSAT pattern: conclusion before its support (vs school's 'conclusion goes last')

named_tools.name: Trojan Horse Concession
named_tools.what: Concessions look like agreement but set up the rebuttal

named_tools.name: Pre-Phrase Goal
named_tools.what: Pronoun-replacement protocol — replace 'such laws' / 'this' / 'they' with actual reference language before stating conclusion

named_tools.name: Skeptic's Ear Check
named_tools.what: R&R prompt: 'What's the simplest question or challenge to this statement?' after each rephrase

named_tools.name: Stegosaurus Interrogation
named_tools.what: Diagnostic technique: ask 'How do I know that?' to tell premise / conclusion / IC apart

voice_passages.passage: HARK, A SIMPLE ARGUMENT
claimy judgments that are usually wrong
Conclusions are worthless and ridiculous like this. They can't keep themselves off the floor. If left to their own devices, conclusions would turn into a hot mess on the floor.
the most salvageable question type ever
the most lovable question type ever
Don't over-complicate this
monsters called cluster sentences
(aghhhhhhhhh)
This is called bad writing. As a reader, you are completely within your rights to call the author out for being a bad writer. As an LSAT test taker, you are called to action.

The purpose of writing is one thing: conveying information to the reader skillfully enough to make it easy for them. This is about sentences that do the opposite. They try to make the reading difficult for you. The test writers purposefully write poorly. They purposefully hide their points in difficult language. In doing so, they create monsters called cluster sentences.
MEMORIZE (TATTOO IT IF YOU HAVE TO)
voice_passages.source: Valid Conclusion Question Mastery SET docx (B)
voice_passages.tightening_note: tighten ALL-CAPS but preserve passage; cap to one occurrence per module

voice_passages.passage: We call these beautiful starships valid conclusions.
voice_passages.source: Valid Conclusion Homework + Note (Homework megadoc, the only place this passage survives)

voice_passages.passage: These whimsical premises keep you from bringing too much outside knowledge into the drill, which would cause you to make possibly unwarranted assumptions without realizing it. We want to train you to stay within whatever the premises happen to be. You have to assume that the premises are true on the LSAT, no matter how outlandish they are. In logic reasoning, the world of the premises is your whole world.
Stay narrow.
Too strong.
Unsupported.
Out of scope.

For the student who is stuck in the 150s or 160s, the most effective strategy would be to study the harder/hardest questions in excruciating detail. The intermediate student already has a fairly comprehensive understanding of the different types of questions, the different approaches required for them, and a rough idea of the knowledge behind different questions. So by looking at the harder questions, we are essentially forcing ourselves to operate at a higher capacity and to analyze the questions on a deeper level. This will not only make us more confident with these harder questions but also make tackling the easier questions a much more manageable process.

Take just as much time to look over the answer choices as the stimulus, if not more. Half the battle to success in Logical Reasoning questions is to differentiate between all the answer choices, choose the best one, and have a clear rationale for your choice.

This exercise is about getting our brains used to the feel of a pattern. Focus on noticing how the first claim is an opinion, and then feeling how the subsequent claims go on to defend that initial opinion.
the govt argues = attribution (usually Opposing); but = Pivot into author's argument; it is clear that = Opinion (usually Conclusion); misguided = Opinion / Rebuttal
you live a very interesting life in this example
Remember all those things your English teachers used to write on your essays?

open_questions.question: The 20 PT-references in MC Companion (PT118 S3 Q12, etc.) point to the external PT bank (v1.5). For v1, do we (a) substitute author-paraphrased stimuli that test the same first-sentence/rebuttal classification, (b) reuse the canonical 20-argument drill from Notes/Homework as the Stage-Gate content (overlap with Module 4 Simulator), or (c) ship Drill 3.4 with placeholder slots and a 'PT bank coming in v1.5' indicator?
open_questions.recommendation: Option (b) — reuse canonical 20 from Notes/Homework. Module 4 Simulator and Drill 3.4 share content but differ in mechanic (Drill 3.4 = Stage-Gate self-grade flow; Simulator = full 5-choice answer set + Coach's Note + Trap Master).

open_questions.topic: Module 4 Simulator answer choices for the 20 canonical questions
open_questions.question: The 20 canonical stimuli have answer keys but several do not have full 5-answer-choice sets in the source files (the source documents focus on classification, not multiple-choice). Spec §4.2 calls for 'Five answer choices (A through E). Sourced from the corpus's full answer-choice sets where available; supplemented with Coach's Note distractors where missing.' Confirm: missing answer choices to be authored by Joshua at Stage 4 build time, or by Claude with Joshua review?
open_questions.recommendation: Joshua authors. The voice and trap-tagging on each distractor is a critical-path authorship task that warrants Joshua's review.

open_questions.question: Drill 3.6 (Design-the-Conclusion) requires AI evaluation of a free-text 'valid conclusion' / 'invalid conclusion' from a whimsical premise pair. v1 = templated (per Gate 1 #5). What's the templated logic? Pre-authored model answers per question with a fuzzy-match similarity check?
open_questions.recommendation: Pre-authored model answers per question (one valid + one invalid) with a meaning-similarity check using local-only sentence embeddings (e.g., transformers.js MiniLM, runs in-browser). No LLM call. Fuzzy match against pre-authored answers earns 'Valid (here's why)' / 'Invalid but interesting' / 'Misses the premises entirely' classification. v1.5 swaps in LLM.

open_questions.question: Intermediate Conclusions & Nested Claims Drill.pdf is image-only OCR'd. Re-OCR with a better engine, or re-author the questions in Markdown using the existing fashion-themed framing?
open_questions.resolution: RESOLVED 2026-04-30 (Joshua): re-OCR first using a vision-model OCR pass. Re-author only if OCR fails. Preserves corpus tier preservation. Plan: scripts/import-content.ts runs vision-model OCR on the source PDF at build time; on failure (confidence threshold or missing fields), the import surfaces a build error and falls back to a re-authored Markdown stub at src/content/drill-3.9-fallback.md (authored only after OCR provably fails).

open_questions.question: Spec §3.6.2 says R&R is desktop-primary with a mobile fallback. What is the mobile fallback's exact shape — same 'cover-and-rephrase' flow per piece without the full-section continuous recording, or no mobile R&R at all in v1?
open_questions.resolution: RESOLVED 2026-04-30 (Joshua): text-only fallback on mobile in v1. Audio-on-mobile is v1.5. Do not block mobile users from R&R. Plan: mobile flow surfaces the R&R protocol (read piece → cover → type rephrase from memory → Skeptic's Ear Check → cumulative recall typed) without microphone capture. Typed responses are the artifact reviewed in the three-column Left Out / Added / Mischaracterized form. Continuous-recording R&R remains desktop-only.

open_questions.topic: Capstone calibration drill content for M1.13 + M5.8
open_questions.question: Per Gate 1 #3, calibration drills draw from corpus + Netlify extractions, NOT external PT bank. M1.13 needs ~10 questions; M5.8 needs ~5 cluster sentences. Do we source these from existing canonical 20 (overlap with Module 4) or curate a separate 'calibration-only' subset?
open_questions.blocker_for: Gate 4 build of M1 + M5 capstones
open_questions.recommendation: Curate a separate calibration-only subset from the canonical 20 (M1.13 = 10 of the 20, varied trait coverage) and from the Cluster Sentences Review walkthrough variants (M5.8 = 5). Mark them in the inventory as `calibration_only: true` so they're not double-counted in Module 4 attempts.
